titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 3 January 1599

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: I receved your letter of the 18th of the last on Christmas day and had aunswered yt sooner yf these holy dayes had affoorded any matter to worke on: only the winde is come about againe for Ireland, and the disgustoItalian and Spanish for displeasure, grievance; the word carried a courtly and diplomatic register. Chamberlain uses it to name — tactfully — the dispute over the cochineal debt that had wrecked Essex's command on Sunday night, 17 December 1598. See Letter 13. that made stay of the erles going for awhile is sweetned and removed, so that now he prepares with all diligence and hath 12000li delivered him to raise five or sixe hundred horse. I cannot come by his particular proportions but as far as I can learne, he is not to have hence above 2000 besides the 2000 that are to come out of the Lowe Countries, which together with the 10000 (in name) that are saide to be there alredy must make up his number.The total of 14,000 soldiers that had originally been proposed for Essex's Irish command (see Letter 12) is now recast: 2,000 fresh troops from England, 2,000 drawn from the Low Countries garrisons, and 10,000 nominally already in Ireland — a paper strength widely understood to be far below the actual field force available. He is so yll satisfied of some that have government there alredy as namely Sir Thomas Maria WingfieldSir Thomas Maria Wingfield, a crown officer in Ireland holding a position of authority over troops and supply; one of those knighted by the departing Lord Deputy Sir William Russell in May 1597. Essex was dissatisfied with several such incumbent officers and intended to replace them wholesale on his arrival. and others, that yt is thought he will make a cleane riddaunce and displace most of them.

Sir Tho. Norris hath lately relieved KillmallokeKilmallock, a fortified town in County Limerick, was a key English stronghold in Munster and came under sustained rebel pressure during the Desmond uprising of 1598–1600. Its relief by Norris was one of the few encouraging scraps of Irish news in this period. a towne in Mounster that was beset and distressed by the enemie: and Sir Samuell Bagnoll hath don some small peece of service upon TironTyrone: a synecdoche for Hugh O'Neill, second Earl of Tyrone, the principal rebel leader. Chamberlain frequently uses the place-name to stand for the man. and brought away three or fowrescore cowes. But the rebells are growne so bold that not long since they fetcht away the Lord ChauncellorsAdam Loftus (c. 1533–1605), Archbishop of Dublin from 1567 and Lord Chancellor of Ireland from 1581; the longest-serving royal official in Elizabethan Ireland. That rebels could drive off the Lord Chancellor's cattle from his own yard in the shadow of St. Patrick's Cathedral — one of the most visible symbols of English Dublin — was a measure of how completely the crown had lost control of the countryside. cattell out of his yarde close by St. Patricks church in the suburbs of Dublin, so that yt were more then time the erle were there, and yet some doubt he shall hardly get hence before St. Georges day.St. George's Day, 23 April, opened the campaigning season. If Essex could not embark before then, the summer would be half-spent before any serious military action could begin.

Sir Robert MansfeldSir Robert Mansell (1573–1656), naval commander; later Vice Admiral of England (1618–1628) and a member of the Virginia Company. His three ships were to patrol the Irish Sea and interdict rebel communications and supplies by sea. goes shortly with three shippes to lie upon that coast. The citie went on reasonable roundly with theyre taske and paid in 50000li the last weeke; the rest is comming after, and yt is thought they shalbe excused with as much more or litle over.The City of London had been asked to lend £150,000 (see Letters 12 and 13); Chamberlain now suggests that paying half — around £50,000 down, with another £50,000–£60,000 to follow — will satisfy the crown's immediate need, and that the city will be excused from the full amount. Now they are to furnish out 600 men for the Lowe Countries within these five or sixe dayes, but that seemes but a fleabiting after the great blowe.

The 21 of the last the Quene signed the patents of Fulke Grivell, Sir Henry Palmer, Trever (the Lord Admiralls secretarie) as Master Treasurer, Controller, and Surveyor of her navie,Fulke Greville (1554–1628), poet and statesman, was appointed Treasurer of the Navy on 21 December 1598, a post he held until 1604. Sir Henry Palmer (c. 1541–1611) became Controller of the Navy. John Trevor became Surveyor of the Navy as the Lord Admiral Nottingham's secretary. The simultaneous appointment of all three officers on the same day was unusual and suggests a deliberate administrative reorganisation of naval governance. and the same time yt is saide she cancelled a bond of 21000li of the erle of Essex, wherin he and a dousen marchants were bound for a cochinille.The cancellation of the cochineal bond — or rather the partial cancellation of it as a gift — was the settlement of the dispute that had blocked Essex's departure in December. See Letter 13: the Queen had promised to forgive £12,000 of his father's debt and £20,000 for cochineal, but she had meant only the forbearance of it. Now a bond of £21,000 is apparently cancelled outright. One Peter BrownePeter Browne, an agent or intelligencer caught intercepting diplomatic correspondence. The Gatehouse was a prison near Westminster Abbey used for state prisoners and those held at the Council's pleasure. is clapt up in the Gatehouse for jugling with the French ambassadors letters, who complaines that his masters letters and his are usually opened before they come at them.

Yet was expected that the treasurship shold have ben bestowed on the Lord Buckhurst for a new-yeares gift, but yt succeded not, so that he must tarry the time, and yt may chaunce light on him on the sodain when he doth least looke for yt, according to the custome of the court that commonly knowes not over night what shalbe don in the morning. I can bethincke me of nothing els, unles yt be the death of Judge OwenThomas Owen (d. 1598), judge of the Court of Common Pleas. He had been mentioned as a possible Master of the Court of Wards — a lucrative post still contested since Lord Burghley's death. His death removes one candidate from the running but does nothing to settle the question. who was in some speach to be master of the wardes. And so I commit you to God.

postscript.p: To my assured goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.
